text: Meetings are, in their truest nature, a collective performance of productivity staged for the benefit of productivity's own absence — a ceremony we conduct to honor the work we are conspicuously not doing. There is something almost liturgical about them: the gathering, the agenda as sacred text, the chairperson as reluctant priest. We speak of "taking away" things from meetings, as though wisdom were a leftover to be boxed up and carried home, when usually what departs with us is merely the warm residue of shared language, the comfortable illusion that speaking and deciding occupy the same room. The meeting ends; the decision, characteristically, schedules a follow-up.
